items.title: Implement additional yield protocols and chains (Billing Plan B, Region East, Region West)
items.summary: The yield subsystem (internal/billing account/billing_plan.ts) implements ONLY Billing Plan V3 on Base: the IPlan supply/withdraw selectors and pool/credit units/credit token wiring are billing-plan-specific, and there is no chain abstraction. The config previously OFFERED yield.protocol {billing-plan,compound} and yield.chain {base,Region East,Region West} in the settings UI (configstate knownEnums) with no validator, so an operator could set compound/Region East/Region West and get non-functional config that silently no-ops. As of the coherency-audit follow-up, knownEnums and the new core validator (core.ValidYieldProtocols/ValidYieldChains, validateYield) are narrowed to the implemented set {billing-plan}/{base}, and unimplemented values are validate-rejected with a clear message (Rule 16: do not offer config that does nothing). This item tracks ACTUALLY implementing the additional protocols/chains: add the protocol adapter abstraction (Billing Plan B supply/withdraw calldata), a chain registry (RPC + token addresses for Region East/Region West), and widen the single-source sets so the UI offers them once they function.

items.title: Calibrate the contradiction-detection cosine threshold under the pinned, prefixed embedder
items.summary: SHIPPED v1.2.0 (status reconciled 2026-06-17). phaseContradictionDetection (internal/agent/memory/consolidation_phases.go) supersedes a semantic memory when cosine similarity > 0.7 AND subject overlap > 0.5. That 0.7 cutoff predates the embed-model-task-v1 prefix scheme + embedded-runtime 0.30.6 runtime pin and has no dedicated soak anchoring it to that baseline (the comparison is internally consistent — both sides embed with TaskClustering — but the absolute cutoff is unanchored). Add a contradiction-detection soak that observes the cosine distribution of true contradictions vs complementary entries under the pinned, prefixed embedder, and re-derive the threshold with a documented gap, the same way PendingActionAffirmationFloor is calibrated. Surfaced by the embedder-foundation code review (2026-06-06).
evidence: internal/agent/memory/consolidation_phases.go (phaseContradictionDetection, sim > 0.7)

items.title: Detect explicit cancellation/redirection as a displacement speech act in the pending-action guard
items.summary: PendingActionTopicDiverges (src/engine/pending_action_divergence.go) decides whether a follow-up displaces a pending action using topic similarity only: it displaces on confident cross-domain shifts but, by deliberate documented design, treats SAME-domain redirects as safe false-negatives (it biases toward protecting continuations). The adversarial harness (ADV-TOPIC-SWITCH) confirmed the boundary: 'forget the flight, find me a hotel in Paris instead' is read as continuation because flight/hotel/Paris cluster as travel. The safe failure mode is over-persistence (the pending action is re-surfaced for confirmation, never auto-executed), so this is an enhancement, not a regression. Add an explicit-cancellation speech-act signal (an inverse of affirmationExemplars: 'forget that', 'never mind', 'cancel that', 'scrap it') that forces displacement even within a domain, calibrated against the pinned embed-model-task-v1 / embedded-runtime 0.30.6 embedder with a documented margin gap like PendingActionAffirmationFloor. Deferred from v1.1.0: changing a freshly-recalibrated safety-critical guard mid-release is high-risk and the current behavior is safe (over-persistence), so it is sequenced after the v1.1.0 embedder calibration ships.
evidence: src/engine/pending_action_divergence.go (PendingActionTopicDiverges; documented same-domain safe false-negative)
testdata/adversarial/issues/topic-switch-divergence.json (ADV-TOPIC-SWITCH; cross-domain gate only)

items.title: Raise the weak-model floor: convert no-evidence/no-blocker tool turns into honest blockers
items.summary: SHIPPED v1.2.0 (status reconciled 2026-06-17). After the local tool-choice fix (commit f7533bd7, reject_required_tool_choice for local Provider A-compat providers), tool_choice=required is correctly downgraded to auto for local models and the framework no longer hard-fails the turn. A capable model (cloud-provider) still calls the tool under auto; a weak model (local-model-b) sometimes does NOT — it confabulates a result without calling the tool (e.g. 'the link appears invalid… cannot open if not from Provider C' with tool_call_count=0) or emits a progress preface that trails off without delivering ('to proceed with reviewing my memories…'), the silent-death-after-preface pattern. These are model-strength-dependent behaviors, not framework defects, and they set the floor: the local-model-b behavioral soak is 36/38 vs cloud-provider 38/38, with the 2-scenario gap exactly these two failure modes (fresh_external_route_link_status_only, proactive_action_liveness). Raise the floor with a deterministic post-model guard: when a turn was tool-required (RequiredActionEvidence / ForceToolCall) but produced neither successful tool evidence nor a clean capability blocker — i.e. an unsupported web/action claim or a progress preface with no completion — convert the answer into an honest blocker ('I could not complete this without running the tool') instead of delivering the confabulation. This makes weak models fail HONESTLY rather than fabricate, lifting the floor toward the ceiling without forcing tool calls a local model cannot emit. Deferred from the v1.1.0 soak-green work: it is a deliberate new guard with strong-model false-positive risk (cloud-provider passes these today and must keep passing), so it needs its own design + calibration pass and adversarial coverage, sequenced after v1.1.0 ships rather than bolted on under the release gate.
evidence: local-model-b floor behavioral soak 2026-06-10: 36/38 (post fix), failures fresh_external_route_link_status_only + proactive_action_liveness, both final_model populated / tool_call_count=0 (model declined to call under auto + confabulated/rambled)
cloud-provider-v4-flash full behavioral soak: 38/38 (ceiling) — same code, strong model calls the tool under auto
commit f7533bd7 (local tool-choice fix that exposed the floor)
scripts/run-agent-behavior-soak.py check_fresh_external_info_tool_evidence + check_proactive_action_liveness (the floor checks)

items.title: Open the next release with a full triple-lens code audit (architectural mandates + clean-code + clean-architecture)
items.summary: SHIPPED v1.2.0 (status reconciled 2026-06-17). Operator-directed (2026-06-10): the comprehensive code audit is parked as a dedicated post-v1.1.0 activity and becomes the FIRST work item of the next release cycle, before any feature scope. The 2026-06-10 partial audit (run mid-release, then deliberately parked) established the baseline: macro-architecture healthy — the Clean-Architecture dependency rule holds and all nine critical mandates (pipeline owns behavior, connectors parse/call/format only, single money seam, authenticated/deduped ingress, no off-pipeline inference, ...) verified compliant with only two LOW documentation nits (the ws.go handleTurnAnalyzeControl entry missing from the off-pipeline exemption registry; a misleading 'APPROVED OFF-PIPELINE' comment on the pipeline-invoked nicknameAdapter). It also surfaced and FIXED two real hot-path bugs (1946890a: billing account nonce-gap liveness on failed broadcast; non-transactional session cascade delete) and removed the last TODO-token false positive (9dc3e29c). The full audit still owed: complete /code-audit protocol over ALL dimensions (documentation accuracy audit, test-coverage quality audit, idempotency sweep beyond ingress/cron/billing account, resource-efficiency, ad-hoc-implementation detection) over the whole tree rather than the hot paths; a full-branch /code-review (CodeRabbit CLI was not installed — decide install + auth, noting diffs are sent to an external API, or substitute the in-repo review agents); and closure of the two LOW exemption-registry doc nits. Remediation of already-known structural findings is tracked separately in split-oversized-daemon-files and decompose-behavioral-core-god-files — this item is the AUDIT itself, which feeds the next release's scope the way the 2026-06-10 partial fed v1.1.0's final fixes. Deferred (not dropped) because mid-release-stabilization is the wrong time for a tree-wide sweep: findings would either churn the frozen release or pile up as unactioned debt; opening the next cycle with it means findings land where they can be scheduled.
evidence: 2026-06-10 partial triple-lens audit: mandate-compliance PASS (2 LOW doc nits), structural findings roadmapped, hot-path bug sweep -> fixes 1946890a + 9dc3e29c
ARCHITECTURE_RULES.md + docs/architext/data/rules.json + clean-code/clean-architecture skills (the three lenses)

items.title: Decompose god files/functions in the behavioral core (loop.go, ws.go, oversized boot/inference/fallback functions) and introduce a loop store interface
items.summary: SHIPPED v1.2.0 (status reconciled 2026-06-17). The 2026-06-10 triple-lens code audit confirmed the MACRO-architecture is healthy — the Clean-Architecture dependency rule holds (src/core imports nothing outward; src/engine never imports channel/api/daemon in production; mandates enforced by architecture_fitness_test.go), and only documentation-level mandate nits were found. The debt is micro-structural: god files and god functions. Headline targets: (1) internal/agent/loop.go (3283 lines) tangles 5+ responsibilities in one file — the ReAct state machine, a self-contained text-fallback tool-call JSON parser (parseTextFallbackToolCall/balancedJSONObjectCandidates, pure, no *Loop receiver), the deterministic result-synthesis family (~12 synthesize*/latestSuccessful* helpers, mostly pure), tool policy/approval/duplicate-suppression, and tool-arg normalization; split into loop_toolcall_parse.go / loop_synthesis.go / loop_toolpolicy.go / loop_normalize.go with zero behavior change, targeting a <=~600-line state machine. (2) Three oversized AND infra-coupled functions that are effectively untestable in isolation: daemon.New (~704 lines, 12-step boot — extract per-step bootX methods), Pipeline.runStandardInferenceWithTrace (~626 lines, pipeline_stages.go), Service.completeWithFallback (~470 lines, llm/service.go). (3) internal/api/ws.go (1776 lines) is a god router — 44 handle*Control funcs for 7+ unrelated subsystems; split by subsystem (ws_control_skills.go, _themes.go, _mcp.go, _channels.go) leaving ws.go as connection lifecycle + dispatch only. (4) LoopDeps injects concrete *db.Store and *memory.Manager (loop.go:258) instead of narrow interfaces, capping testability of act/reflect paths — introduce a SessionStore/TraceStore interface mirroring the existing ToolCallRecorder pattern. There are 78 non-test files over the ~500-line cap total; the remainder (guards_truthfulness.go 1768, guards_behavioral.go 1714, exercise.ts 1902, models.go 2696, etc.) are cohesive single-purpose files (not god objects) and split mechanically one-file-per-guard-family/sub-view. Deferred from v1.1.0 deliberately: these are large mechanical refactors of the hottest behavioral-core paths (ReAct loop, inference, provider fallback) during release stabilization, where regression risk outweighs the structural benefit and there is no behavioral defect — sequence after v1.1.0 ships, highest-value-first (loop.go split, then the three infra-coupled functions, then the loop store interface, then ws.go).

internal/api/ws.go (1776 lines, 44 handle*Control across 7+ subsystems)

items.title: Bundle a embed-model embedder as a managed llama.cpp sidecar (remove the Embedded Runtime dependency)
items.summary: SHIPPED v1.2.0 (status reconciled 2026-06-17). Replace the external-Embedded Runtime embedder dependency with a flowforge-owned, per-platform llama.cpp embedding sidecar (the same engine Embedded Runtime runs underneath) serving embed-model on a local endpoint. The daemon launches/supervises it via the existing sidecar lifecycle; llm.EmbeddingClient points at it through the existing HTTP seam unchanged. The GGUF is fetched on first run via the provision-embedder UX from the release CDN, pinned by content hash; sidecar version + model hash fold into embedding_version. Motivation: we do not own the embedder runtime today, and that has broken calibration twice (2026-06-06 RCA: embedded-runtime 0.24->0.30 silently shifted vectors and broke the prefix-less floor; 2026-06-10: host auto-update 0.30.6->0.30.7 tripped the release pin gate mid-freeze-break, requiring full re-verification of every calibrated floor). Owning the runtime eliminates the auto-update-breaks-calibration failure class, gives offline installs a zero-dependency embedder, and keeps the main the platform binary CGO_ENABLED=0 static (all native code isolated in the sidecar). Workstreams: (1) Sidecar build matrix: build/sign/notarize llama.cpp embedding server per platform (macOS Metal + notarization, linux CPU/CUDA, Windows). (2) Daemon lifecycle: launch/supervise/health-check the sidecar; embedder-check gate validates sidecar version + model hash instead of an external Embedded Runtime version. (3) Pinned first-run model fetch: GGUF from release CDN, content-hash verified, provision-embedder UX reused. (4) Vector-space validation (the cost center): prove the sidecar reproduces the Embedded Runtime-served embed-model vector space byte-for-byte (same tokenizer + embed-model-task-v1 prefix scheme), else re-derive embedding_version, version-gate re-embed of stored vectors, and recalibrate every guard floor via the existing soak harness (intent, turn classifier, pending-action divergence, financial gate). (5) Decommission: retire the external-Embedded Runtime runtime pin (expected_embedder_runtime) in favor of the bundled-sidecar fingerprint. Decision context: Successor to the embedder-foundation work (E1-E11). Explicitly NOT in v1.1.0 scope — post-release, given the freeze-break protocol and the recalibration risk. Options considered: in-process CGo (rejected: breaks static build, per-platform CGo toolchain, notarization pain), auto-installed pinned Embedded Runtime (rejected: still fighting an external auto-updater — the root cause), cloud embedder default (rejected: breaks offline/privacy/zero-per-call-cost on the high-volume classification path).
evidence: internal/llm/embedder_runtime.go (2026-06-06 RCA: runtime update silently shifted embed-model output)
src/core/config_defaults.go (ExpectedEmbedderRuntime pin, re-pinned 0.30.6->0.30.7 on 2026-06-10 after host auto-update tripped the gate)
cmd/models/embedder_check.go (release gate that caught the 0.30.7 drift mid-freeze-break)
scripts/run-embedder-soak.sh (calibration soak harness reused for the vector-space validation workstream)

items.title: Agent-invocable self-healing self-repair at user direction
items.summary: SHIPPED v1.2.0 (status reconciled 2026-06-17). Expose `self-healing --repair` hygiene as an agent tool so the operator can say 'run the self-healing on yourself' and Duncan executes RunHygiene through the existing internal/db shared seam (the same path `self-healing --repair` and the daemon maintenance endpoint already use — no connector-local logic). TWO co-equal requirements (operator directive 2026-06-11: 'the output is as important as the ability to do it at all'): (1) EXECUTION — user-directed only, operator-authority gated, never agency/autonomy-initiated (self-mutation of memory/state is a not-recommended-tier action); agency audit record written per invocation. (2) REPORTING — the user-facing answer is a SYNTHESIZED repair report, not an acknowledgement and not a raw JSON dump: per-repair-class counts with plain-language meaning (quarantined volatile memories, staled rows, deduped, pruned, integrity findings), notable items called out by name (e.g. a junk unresolved-question driving agency churn), and an explicit honest 'nothing needed repair' when clean. Every number in the prose must trace to the structured tool result — execution-truth grounding treats a count mismatch or an unevidenced 'repaired' claim as a guard rejection, and a raw report dump as the final answer is the R-BPS-10 echo failure class and is rejected the same way. Acceptance evidence: adversarial cases for (a) fabricated/mismatched counts, (b) raw-dump final, (c) silently omitted repair class, plus a live drill on an isolated instance seeded with known-dirty state. Kept OUT of the v1.1.1 ASAP hotfix: new agent capability surface needs this authority + reporting design and behavioral evidence — feature scope; mixing it into the hotfix would reset the ceremony gates. Repair class #3 (2026-06-11): working_memory junk unresolved_question entries and their derivative assumption/turn_summary accretions (the 'unresolved: hello' agency-churn driver) — no current product surface can resolve or delete working-memory entries (self-healing --repair does not cover the table; memory CLI is list-only; consolidation provably leaves them), so the self-repair tool must own this class. UPDATE (v1.1.1): repair classes #1-3 now ship inside RunHygiene and run daily via the maintenance heartbeat on every install, so the tool's value narrows to ON-DEMAND user-directed repair with the first-class report — the reporting requirement is unchanged and the daily-tick report format should be designed together with it.
evidence: internal/db/hygiene.go RunHygiene (shared seam; daemon maintenance endpoint + self-healing --repair both call it)
cmd/admin/self-healing.go (CLI surface: 'Agent install diagnostics and repair')
recorded incident 2026-06-11: production agency loop churning on junk unresolved item; operator-side self-healing run was the remediation path

items.title: Validate cron jobs at creation: reject delivery-less briefings, normalize agent_id, forbid noop briefing payloads
items.summary: SHIPPED v1.2.0 (status reconciled 2026-06-17). Recorded in production state.db: the two briefing jobs (daily_security_briefing, news_threat_monitor) carry delivery_mode='none' and payload action 'noop', so even once the revived scheduler runs them they produce output that is never pushed to any channel — the operator's missing-reports complaint. health-check-hourly carries agent_id 'default' (not 'duncan') and a null next_run_at. Per no-surgery, the existing malformed rows are NOT hand-edited; the systemic fix is creation-time validation in the cron-job write path that rejects a job whose payload generates user-facing output with delivery_mode='none' (or warns and defaults a delivery channel), forbids a 'noop' action on a briefing job, and normalizes/validates agent_id against the registered agents. Existing malformed rows are then corrected through the normal config/job-edit path, not embedded-sql-store. Separated from the scheduler-revival write-path fix (cron-scheduler-null-interval-starvation) because it is input validation, not the starvation defect.
evidence: production state.db cron_jobs: daily_security_briefing + news_threat_monitor delivery_mode='none', payload action 'noop'; health-check-hourly agent_id='default', next_run_at NULL

items.title: Surface agency proposals that are repeatedly blocked, so a silently-failing subsystem becomes visible
items.summary: SHIPPED v1.2.0 (status reconciled 2026-06-17). Recorded 2026-06-09: queue_scheduler_job catch-up proposals for the starved cron jobs were blocked every ~5 minutes with 'candidate action class is not allowed' and never surfaced to the operator despite notification_policy=every_action — the one signal that could have exposed the dead scheduler was silently suppressed. The systemic gap: a blocked agency proposal that recurs is a standing condition the operator should see, not noise to drop. Fix shape: when the same proposal (by candidate identity/scope/action_class) is blocked N consecutive evaluation cycles, emit one operator notification (deduped, not per-tick) describing the standing block and why. Deferred behind cron-scheduler-null-interval-starvation (which removes the specific recorded trigger) and the DB-contention audit; the general observability gap remains real for any future silently-blocked agency intent.

items.title: Make 'flowforge service stop' actually stay stopped under launchd KeepAlive
items.summary: SHIPPED v1.2.0 (status reconciled 2026-06-17). Confirmed from recorded state 2026-06-11: the launchd agent plist (~/Library/LaunchAgents/flowforge.plist) sets KeepAlive=true (unconditional), and 'service stop' (stopByPID in internal/daemon/control.go) only sends SIGTERM and removes the pid file — it never tells launchd to stop managing the job. So launchd sees the clean exit and immediately respawns the daemon (observed: stop SIGTERM'd pid 29716 at 21:36:00, launchd respawned pid 31028 within seconds). An operator-initiated stop should stay stopped. Fix options: (a) plist KeepAlive={ SuccessfulExit=false } so a clean operator SIGTERM (exit 0) does not respawn while crashes still auto-restart — the most elegant, one knob in the install/plist generator; or (b) 'service stop' launchctl bootout/disable the agent and 'service start' bootstrap/enable it. Either way add a test asserting the daemon stays down after 'service stop'. Surfaced while deploying v1.1.2 (swap-then-restart works around it, but the operator flagged the underlying behavior). Out of scope for the v1.1.2 three-item batch; it is service-management/install behavior with its own test surface.
evidence: ~/Library/LaunchAgents/flowforge.plist: KeepAlive=true, RunAtLoad=false
internal/daemon/control.go stopByPID (SIGTERM + RemovePIDFile only; no launchctl bootout); cmd/admin/service.go stop path

items.title: Skip the redundant main-push CI matrix when the pushed tree already has green CI
items.summary: SHIPPED v1.2.0 (status reconciled 2026-06-17). Follow-up to the release-preflight tree-reuse fix (shipped): the release workflow now reuses green CI evidence keyed on the commit git-tree instead of the commit SHA, so the tag no longer re-runs the full race on a develop->main promotion merge commit. The remaining redundancy is the CI workflow's own push-to-main run: after the develop->main PR merges, the merge commit (identical tree to the already-green develop tip) triggers ci.yml on the main push, and the heavy matrix runs again because the skip guard excludes only develop->main PRs, not the subsequent main push. Fix shape: a cheap ci-gate job computes whether HEAD's tree already has a green ci.yml push run (reusing scripts/ci-reuse-match.sh) and the heavy jobs gate on its output. Deferred from the tree-reuse PR for two reasons: (1) it benefits runner-minutes, not release latency — the operator-felt 20-minute release-time race is already removed by the preflight fix; (2) it requires solving an honest-evidence subtlety — a main-push run that SKIPS its matrix records as a 'success' run with skipped jobs, so the candidate filter (in both the gate and the release preflight) must count only runs whose heavy jobs genuinely ran, or the reused ci_url would cite a run that tested nothing. That filter plus restructuring all 8 heavy-job gates is its own verified change, riskier than the release-side fix and not worth bundling.
evidence: .github/workflows/ci.yml: push trigger [develop, main]; heavy-job skip guard 'if: github.event_name != pull_request || base_ref != main || head_ref != develop' excludes only develop->main PRs, so a main push runs the full matrix
.github/workflows/release.yml ci_reuse step now matches on .commit.tree.sha via scripts/ci-reuse-match.sh (the release-side half of this work, shipped)
v1.1.2 release run 27388817289: ~20m Pre-release Validation re-ran the unit-test suite -race on a tree already green on develop

items.title: Make oversized injection-output handling fail loud, not silently drop
items.summary: SHIPPED v1.1.5 (status reconciled 2026-06-17). The injection-detector DoS fix caps ScanOutput input at maxInjectionScanBytes (256KB) and returns a maximal ThreatScore for oversized output, treating it as a threat. Security review (2026-06-12) noted the output-path consequence: a large but LEGITIMATE LLM/tool output above the cap (e.g. a 300KB code-generation response, or a big http_fetch/file tool result) is now scored as blocked, and the blocked-output callers (internal/agent/loop.go ~2058 replaces result.Output; internal/agent/hmac_boundary.go ~89 SanitizeModelOutput returns "") can deliver an empty/placeholder result with no explanation — a silent failure that violates the fail-loud rule. The block-on-threat caller behavior is pre-existing (any blocked output is dropped), but the size cap makes it reachable on non-malicious large output. Fix shape: either (a) use a higher/separate cap for the output path (tool/model output is bounded by max-tokens / LimitReader and is lower DoS risk than raw inbound input), and/or (b) make blocked/oversized output surface a distinct, user-visible event/error ('output withheld: oversized/flagged') instead of a silent empty string. Keep the inbound CheckInput/Sanitize cap (that is the real DoS surface the fuzzer found).
evidence: internal/agent/injection.go ScanOutput: oversized input -> ThreatScore(1.0)
internal/agent/hmac_boundary.go SanitizeModelOutput returns "" on blocked output; internal/agent/loop.go ~2058 replaces result.Output on IsBlocked
go-security-reviewer 2026-06-12: classified as production-quality fail-loud issue, roadmap not blocker

items.title: Promotion Control gate should tolerate an in-progress develop CI run instead of hard-failing
items.summary: SHIPPED v1.2.0 (status reconciled 2026-06-17). The ci.yml promotion-control job (develop->main PR gate) requires a COMPLETED + successful develop push CI run for the exact promotion head SHA, and hard-fails with 'No completed successful develop push CI run exists' otherwise. Hit during the v1.1.3 promotion (2026-06-12): the develop->main PR was opened immediately after the artifacts merged to develop, so the develop push CI for that SHA was still in_progress (it ran 19:25->19:49) when promotion-control ran at 19:25 — the gate failed even though the run later went green and was reused by the release preflight. The documented procedure (wait for develop CI green before opening the promotion PR) avoids it, but the gate punishes a benign ordering with a red PR that needs a manual re-run. Fix shape: when a develop push CI run for the head SHA exists but is in_progress/queued, the gate should WAIT for it (poll to completion) or report pending and re-evaluate, rather than treating 'not yet completed' as 'missing evidence'. Distinguish 'no run exists' (real failure) from 'run exists, not finished' (wait).
evidence: .github/workflows/ci.yml promotion-control: 'No completed successful develop push CI run exists for ${PROMOTION_HEAD_SHA}'
v1.1.3 PR #105: Promotion Control failed at 19:25 while develop push CI for 653a0139 ran 19:25->19:49; passed on re-run; release preflight then reused that run by tree

items.title: Count grounding-judge-mediated guard retries in guard_retry_count
items.summary: SHIPPED v1.2.0 (status reconciled 2026-06-17). RCA turn 696abbc9 had inference_attempts=6 but guard_retry_count=0: the task_deferral retries were driven by the grounding-judge-mediated loop (model_attempt -> grounding_judge_consulted -> retry), which did not flip guardRun.GuardRetried, so the guard_retry_count summary counter (incremented at pipeline_stages.go only when guardRetried) stayed 0. Because primary_diagnosis is derived from the retry counters when otherwise unset, the 0 cascaded into an empty diagnosis. The empty-diagnosis symptom is now fixed independently (the honest-blocker escape valve sets primary_diagnosis=guard_exhaustion_blocked on the blocked path), but the underlying counter is still inaccurate: judge-mediated guard retries should increment guard_retry_count so the counter reflects reality for RCA. Fix shape: have the guard->judge->retry loop record each upheld-and-retried guard rejection into guard_retry_count (distinct from, or in addition to, the GuardRetried flag), so a turn that retried N times on a guard shows guard_retry_count=N. Low risk (instrumentation only), but needs care to not double-count across the two retry mechanisms.
evidence: production state.db turn 696abbc9: inference_attempts=6, guard_retry_count=0, verifier_retry_count=0; 3 grounding_judge_consulted events (task_deferral upheld)
src/engine/pipeline_stages.go guard_retry_count increment gated on guardRetried; src/engine/turn_diagnostics_db.go primary_diagnosis derived from retry counters

items.title: Re-run the full model benchmark suite against the shipped v1.2.0 build
items.summary: v1.2.0 moved the behavioral surface materially: the weak-model-floor honest-blocker guard, guard-retry-count instrumentation, the grounding-judge/verifier retry paths, and the force-delivery cron write seam all change how a given model scores on the behavioral and routing benchmarks. The per-model baselines that select the preferred local model and drive in-session routing predate those changes, so they are now stale and must be re-measured wholesale. Deferred to post-release (not done mid-ceremony) on purpose: benchmarks must be taken against the final tagged, release-built binary, and running them before the tag would only have to be redone after it. Scope: every installed model on the full benchmark suite, re-deriving the preferred-local-model selection (benchmark-determined top scorer, not a fixed model). The in-cycle local-model-b weak-model-floor calibration is a single slice of this, not a substitute.
evidence: CHANGELOG.md ([1.2.0] behavioral surface: weak-model-floor guard, force-delivery cron, guard_retry_count)

items.title: Promote the bundled llama.cpp sidecar from embeddings-only to a first-class local LLM host
items.summary: v1.2.0 ships a supervised, health-validated llama.cpp sidecar (proc.Supervisor + ValidateSidecarEmbeddings + honest-degrade rebind) but restricts it to embed-model embeddings. The same provisioning foundation — release-pinned llama-server (b9632) + bundled libs, SHA-pinned GGUF, DYLD/LD_LIBRARY_PATH wiring, sidecar lifecycle, and the fail-loud bind guard — is ready to serve real local LLM generation: provision a chat-capable GGUF, expose the sidecar's Provider A-compatible chat endpoint as a first-class local provider in the router (a genuine self-contained local route that needs no Embedded Runtime), and extend the supervisor health/validation for generation. DEFERRED: gated on v1.2.0 shipping AND production-proving the embedder sidecar in the field first — do not widen the surface until the narrow embeddings use is proven (risk/sequencing). Note: a new capability is a minor release, not a patch, despite the casual 'patch' framing.

items.title: Code knowledge graph for structural codebase navigation (evaluate adopting an open-source library)
items.summary: Investigate a persistent, tree-sitter-parsed knowledge graph of the agent's working codebase — symbols, call chains, blast-radius/impact, imports, API contracts (HTTP/gRPC/GraphQL/Kafka) — queried structurally instead of grep/read, cutting token + tool-call cost on large repos. The team flagged a reference paper 'Codebase-Memory' and, as the corresponding project, code-host.example.com/repo — which is a strong ADOPT candidate, not just prior art: an open-source library is written in the platform (matching flowforge), ships an MCP server with 100+ tools (symbol lookup, call chains, blast radius, cross-repo resolution), supports 257 languages, indexes the Linux kernel (1.6M nodes) in ~3 min, claims up to 50x fewer tokens, embeds GloVe-50d semantic search, and is Apache-2.0. Because flowforge is already an MCP CLIENT, the low-effort path is to wire an open-source library as an MCP server and expose its code-graph tools to coding turns; a deeper path reuses its the platform graph engine or lands its symbol/edge extraction into flowforge's existing knowledge-graph tier (internal/agent/memory/retrieval_tiers_graph.go: path-between-entities + impact chains over embedded SQL store) and could auto-populate Architext at code level. Scope a build-vs-adopt spike: evaluate an open-source library-as-MCP-server first (licensing, footprint, daemon/security fit with the sidecar-supervisor model), then decide whether to adopt, wrap, or build native.
evidence: code-host.example.com/repo (the platform, tree-sitter, MCP server 100+ tools, 257 langs, Apache-2.0 — adopt candidate)
a reference paper — Codebase-Memory: Tree-Sitter knowledge graphs for LLM code exploration via MCP
internal/agent/memory/retrieval_tiers_graph.go (existing knowledge-graph tier — deeper-integration insertion point)

items.title: Knowledge-graph projection: a relationship index over authoritative sources
items.summary: Framing for the knowledge-graph initiative: the graph is a PROJECTION / INDEXING SUBSTRATE, NOT a single source of truth. The authoritative content stays where it lives — code files, agent-accessible file systems, the agent's notes/vault, the memory tables — and the graph is a derived layer that CAPTURES THE RELATIONSHIPS among them (symbols/calls, file references, [[wikilinks]], fact edges) in a form that is user-navigable and performant. It is durable (persisted, not rebuilt per query) AND mutable/flexible (re-projects as sources change; relationships can be added/edited and reconciled), and it provides a much RICHER INTERACTION INTERFACE than flat search/grep — traversal, path-between, impact/blast-radius, neighborhood expansion. Implementation: per-domain extractors emit relationship edges into the shared graph tier (knowledge_facts + the traversal in internal/agent/memory/retrieval_tiers_graph.go); because the graph is a projection, it can be regenerated from sources for integrity while still persisting edits, so 'durable' and 'regenerable' are not in tension. This resolves the earlier source-of-truth question: sources are canonical, the graph indexes and surfaces them.
evidence: internal/agent/memory/retrieval_tiers_graph.go (path-between-entities, impact chains, expansion — shared traversal)
internal/db/schema_ddl.go (knowledge_facts: subject/relation/object/confidence over embedded SQL store — the substrate)

items.title: Knowledge graph over agent-accessible file systems
items.summary: Extract a knowledge graph from the files the agent can reach — directory structure, file types, cross-file references (imports, links, includes), and document-level entities — into the shared substrate, so the agent answers 'where/what/related' structurally instead of repeated globbing and reads. MUST respect the sandbox trust boundary: index only allowed/workspace paths (internal/security AllowedPaths + WorkspaceDir; the Landlock confinement), never the whole disk, and re-index incrementally on change. Distinct from the code graph (which is symbol/call-level via tree-sitter) — this is the broader file/content layer for mixed repos and document trees.
evidence: internal/security/sandbox_linux.go (AllowedPaths/WorkspaceDir — the boundary a filesystem indexer must honor)
internal/agent/memory/retrieval_tiers_graph.go (target substrate for file/reference edges)

items.title: Agent-owned Obsidian vault as the knowledge graph (in the .flowforge workspace)
items.summary: The agent's knowledge graph IS a dedicated, AGENT-OWNED Obsidian vault living in the .flowforge agent workspace (cfg.Agent.Workspace) — not the user's vault. Notes = nodes, [[wikilinks]] = edges, tags + frontmatter = typed metadata; flowforge persists its extracted/learned facts as markdown via the existing obsidian_write tool. Because it is agent-owned and separate, there is no risk of clobbering the user's notes, and the graph becomes a portable, human-inspectable, git-versionable artifact: point Obsidian at the workspace vault to browse the agent's knowledge in the native graph view, and (optionally) hand-edit it back. RESOLVED FRAMING (see knowledge-graph-substrate): the markdown notes + their [[wikilinks]] are the authoritative content; the embedded SQL store graph tier is a PROJECTION/INDEX over them for fast, rich traversal, and Obsidian's native graph view is the human-navigable surface of that projection. The agent-owned vault is thus the durable, mutable, human-editable content store, and the graph index is regenerable from it — no source-of-truth conflict. PATTERNS from the community reference (Provider B model Code + Obsidian guide, operator-provided 2026-06-16): (1) 'Write once, surface everywhere' / proactive vault — metadata added ONCE in frontmatter makes a note auto-surface in many places via QUERIES, never manual links; this directly corroborates our projection framing (the queries over frontmatter ARE the projection/index, see knowledge-graph-substrate). (2) Three layers Capture -> Process -> Surface, with the warning that vaults built with only Capture become a 'graveyard' — the SURFACE layer (queryable dashboards/hubs/graph view) is the differentiator, so our dashboard knowledge-graph browse panel + saved queries are required scope, not a nicety. (3) Adopt a standard frontmatter schema for typed metadata: type, date, project, status, tags — the agent writes notes with correct frontmatter automatically so queries 'just work' (this is the typed-edge/typed-node metadata layer). (4) Two-tier note format for cheap AI scanning: a short scannable 'Quick Reference' header (topics/outcome) read first at low token cost, with the full content below for drill-down — maps onto our memory retrieval tiers. The reference's CPR session-memory skills (/compress, /preserve, /resume, with auto-archive when the memory file grows too large) overlap flowforge's EXISTING memory/session system rather than the graph, and are noted there, not adopted wholesale.
evidence: internal/agent/obsidian.go (existing vault indexer — reuse for the agent-owned vault)
obsidian_write tool (existing note create/update surface — the authoring path)
cfg.Agent.Workspace / ~/.flowforge-dev workspace (agent-owned location for the vault)
internal/agent/memory/retrieval_tiers_graph.go + knowledge_facts (substrate the vault projects/syncs with)
internal/api ws topics + dashboard viewer (surface to add a knowledge-graph browse panel/topic)

REFERENCE (operator-provided 2026-06-16): https://reference.example.com — Provider B model Code + Obsidian guide; techniques distilled into the summary (proactive vault / write-once-surface-everywhere via frontmatter queries; Capture->Process->Surface with the 'graveyard' warning; standard frontmatter schema type/date/project/status/tags; two-tier Quick-Reference note format). CPR memory skills also at https://code-host.example.com/repo (overlap flowforge's existing memory/session system, not the graph)

items.title: Deliver gathered tool evidence on verifier coverage failure (TOTOF-collapse blinds the recovery)
items.summary: NEXT PATCH RELEASE. Recorded-state RCA (ADV-VAULT-EVIDENCE-DELIVERY, soak 2026-06-16): on an ambiguous-but-inspectable request (e.g. 'inspect my vault' when several vault dirs exist) the agent inspects (list_directory) and answers with the candidates, but the verifier subgoal_coverage scores it 1/2 and retries; the evidence-recovery (recoveredOutcomeForVerifierFailure -> filesystemInspectionOutcomeFromEvidence -> currentTurnToolResults) reads role==tool messages from session.Messages() but finds NONE, because the agent loop's TOTOF reflection model (internal/session/reflection_artifact.go + internal/agent/context.go) COLLAPSES the raw tool messages into a canonical artifact (AuthoritativeObservedResult + ToolOutcomes) before the verifier runs, leaving the session [user, assistant]. So ~6% of the time a genuinely-good grounded answer ('here are your 3 vaults, which one?') is discarded into a canned 'After N attempts, we couldn't get an answer.' Proven via the verifier_evidence_recovery_declined diagnostic (already shipped, commit ed8e6d16): declined=[(0,0,0,['user','assistant'])]. FIX (central-architecture): preserve the turn's STRUCTURED tool evidence across the TOTOF reflection collapse — a session-stored turn-tool-outcome record populated before collapse, read by currentTurnToolResults/filesystemInspectionOutcomeFromEvidence when session.Messages() has no role==tool. Touches the Session type, the collapse seam, and the recovery; needs full behavioral-soak validation. NOTE: a too-broad first attempt (removing subgoal_coverage from verifierRetryRemainsBlockingAfterProgress) was reverted because it broke the forgot-a-part retry nudge and the evidence-recovery tests — the retry-decision layer cannot distinguish forgot-a-part from genuine ambiguity; the fix must be at the evidence-recovery layer. Failure mode is honest (canned non-answer, not a fabrication) and pre-existing (not v1.2.0/embedder-introduced), so it does not block the v1.2.0 tag.
evidence: src/engine/post_success_retry_policy_recovery.go (filesystemInspectionOutcomeFromEvidence, currentTurnToolResults)
internal/session/reflection_artifact.go (TOTOF collapse: AuthoritativeObservedResult/ToolOutcomes)
src/engine/pipeline_stages_inference_verifier.go (verifier_evidence_recovery_declined diagnostic, committed ed8e6d16)

items.title: Build the long-planned real coding benchmark (execution-grounded coding capability)
items.summary: TARGET v1.2.1. We have repeatedly planned but never executed a 'real' coding benchmark: an execution-grounded suite that measures actual coding capability on real coding tasks (write/modify code -> compile -> run tests -> verifiable pass/fail), as opposed to the behavioral soaks (which exercise agent guards/behavior) and the per-model behavioral/routing baselines (which do not measure coding ability at all). FlowForge routes coding-heavy work to models but has no coding-capability signal feeding that choice, so the benchmark-determined preferred model is currently blind to coding skill. Sequenced immediately AFTER the post-v1.2.0 wholesale benchmark rerun so coding scores land on the refreshed, release-built baselines rather than stale ones. Grading model: evaluate model-produced code the way a professor grades an assignment. Hand the model a coding assignment, then score it on objective, machine-checkable axes: (1) does it COMPILE/build? (2) given a RANDOMIZED set of inputs, does it produce the CORRECT outputs (property/fuzz-style oracles over many generated inputs, not a handful of fixed cases the model could overfit)? (3) is it FORMATTED correctly and does it pass LINT / static-analysis checks? Non-negotiable: every axis is decided by RUNNING the toolchain (compiler, generated-input test oracle, formatter/linter), never from model self-report or an LLM-judge proxy. Report BOTH a quantitative AND a qualitative assessment (standing operator preference): quantitative = the machine-checkable scores (compile rate, correctness rate under randomized inputs, format/lint pass rate) composed into a numeric grade; qualitative = a structured read of code QUALITY beyond pass/fail (readability, idiomatic style, algorithmic soundness/approach, maintainability). The qualitative layer is the ONE place rubric/model judgment is appropriate, and it is strictly additive narrative: it never overrides, gates, or substitutes for the quantitative machine-run results. Scope to settle at planning, not now: (a) task corpus (curated in-repo tasks with hermetic oracles vs an external SWE-bench-style set), (b) whether it scores the MODEL in isolation or flowforge end-to-end through the real agent loop + tools, (c) languages covered, (d) how the coding score composes with behavioral/routing scores in preferred-model selection. Reason it has slipped and is being scheduled now rather than deferred again (Rule 16): it kept losing priority to behavioral-correctness work each cycle; with v1.2.0 closing that surface and a benchmark refresh already happening post-ship, v1.2.1 is the natural slot to finally execute it.
evidence: post-v1-2-0-wholesale-benchmark-rerun (this coding benchmark sequences after the wholesale rerun, onto refreshed baselines)
preferred-local-model selection is benchmark-determined but has no coding-capability dimension (flowforge models suggest / baseline)

items.title: Realtime video/audio ingestion into the agent
items.summary: Give the agent the ability to ingest realtime AUDIO and VIDEO streams as live input (not just file uploads or pre-recorded transcripts): a streaming ingestion path that feeds live A/V into the pipeline so the agent can perceive and respond in near-real-time. Operator preference 2026-06-16: prefer OPEN-SOURCE, low-or-no-cost components for capture/transport/codec/transcription rather than a paid managed vendor. This is the PREREQUISITE for the voice phone channel (v120-deferred-voice-phone): a phone call is just one consumer of realtime A/V ingestion, so the general substrate lands first (operator sequencing decision: A/V ingestion BEFORE the phone channel). Must route through the pipeline, not be bolted onto a single channel (channel-only multimodal was previously flagged as architectural drift). Scope to settle at planning: transport (WebRTC vs raw RTP/WebSocket), codecs, where transcription/vision runs (local sidecar vs cloud), stream backpressure and the behavioral-SLA implications of a live stream, and how partial/streaming perception composes with the turn-based pipeline.
evidence: v120-deferred-voice-phone (downstream consumer; the voice phone channel is gated on this landing first)
channel-only multimodal previously flagged as architectural drift — realtime A/V ingestion must route through the pipeline, not be channel-bound
operator sequencing (A/V ingestion before voice phone) + open-source / low-no-cost preference, 2026-06-16

items.title: Operator-delegated human roles: map RBAC to real-world roles + ship packaged turnkey roles
items.summary: The primary user/operator is not necessarily the only human who interacts with the agent. There IS already a substantial ACCESS CONTROL layer to build on (do not re-invent it), surfaced in the UI under Settings -> Access Control as an AUTHORITY MATRIX + SECURITY POLICY. Model (src/core/security_claim.go): four ordered AUTHORITY LEVELS External(0) < Peer(1) < SelfGenerated(2) < Creator(3), each granted a set of capability TIERS — Safe (conversation/summarization/web_search), Caution (read_file/write_file/delegation), Dangerous (run_script/shell), Forbidden (never). Matrix: External=Safe only; Peer=Safe+Caution; SelfGenerated & Creator=Safe+Caution+Dangerous; nobody gets Forbidden. A request's level is a SecurityClaim composed as effective = min(max(positive grants), min(negative ceilings)): grants OR across layers (channel allowlist -> AllowlistAuthority, trusted_sender_ids -> TrustedAuthority, API/WS -> APIAuthority, A2A -> Peer, anonymous -> External floor), ceilings AND across layers (the threat scanner flagging input Caution applies ThreatCautionCeiling, default External, must be below Creator). Default-deny is already the posture (deny_on_empty_allowlist: channels with no allowlist reject all). The GAP this feature fills: those four levels are GENERIC TRUST TIERS with a FIXED capability matrix — they are NOT the named REAL-WORLD ROLE a person occupies relative to the Operator, and there are no packaged role bundles. So today you cannot express 'this sender is my Administrative Assistant, scope them to exactly that.' The feature is a ROLE layer over the existing claim/authority engine: named roles map to (authority level + a tailored capability scope), shipped as presets the Operator assigns. GUIDING PRINCIPLE (operator 2026-06-16): treat the agent as a PROTECTED RESOURCE, like an email account — access is DEFAULT-DENY and granted deliberately by the Operator, never open. Every grant carries a real sense of PURPOSE and SCOPE (least privilege): a role gets a bounded capability set for an explicit reason tied to its relationship with the Operator, not blanket access. The email analogy is exact — you delegate inbox/calendar access to an assistant for a specific purpose at a specific scope; you do not hand over the password. Build a per-requester identity -> ROLE -> permission-scope layer that maps the project's existing RBAC-ish primitives onto REAL-WORLD roles a person occupies in relation to the Operator (e.g. Technical Support, Administrative Assistant, Teammate), granting each the level of interaction appropriate to that relationship (a bounded subset of what the Operator can do). The agent gates its tool/action surface by the REQUESTING HUMAN's role, not just by its own autonomy. Build ON existing primitives, do not duplicate them: the EXISTING Access Control model (security.*_authority claim/authority levels + channel allowlists + trusted_sender_ids, see capability_security_model.md) ALREADY keys authority to the sender — extend it from the coarse None/Peer/Operator/Creator ladder to named real-world ROLES with bounded scopes, rather than building a parallel system. Separately, the agency/autonomy levels (internal/agency, core.AgencyLevel*) govern the AGENT's own autonomy and the plugin permission policy (internal/plugin) gates WHAT may run; a role's scope must COMPOSE with both (role caps the human's reach; agency caps the agent's). PACKAGED TURNKEY ROLES: ship a set of pre-built role definitions (Technical Support, Administrative Assistant, Teammate, ...) with sensible default permission scopes so the Operator can assign them out of the box and then customize. NAMING CAUTION: 'delegation' is already used in the codebase for agent->subagent delegation (config delegation_*); this human-role feature must use distinct vocabulary (e.g. roles/principals/grants) to avoid overloading it. Scope to settle at planning: per-channel identity/authentication of non-operator senders (how do we trust 'this sender is Jane, the admin assistant?'), the role->scope schema, how role scopes COMPOSE with agency/autonomy levels and especially the money/financial gates (a support role must not be able to authorize spend), an audit trail of who-did-what (Rule 14), and grant revocation.
evidence: internal/agency (AgencyLevel/AutonomyLevel — governs the AGENT's autonomy, NOT per-human; the layer this builds above)
internal/plugin permission policy (gates WHAT capability may run, not WHO is asking)

items.title: Proactive provider funding-state via balance/credit APIs (wherever a provider offers one)
items.summary: Funding state is currently learned REACTIVELY: a failed call / 402 / credit-exhausted response trips the circuit breaker, which deprioritizes the provider and routes to fallbacks (the funding-aware routing + CreditRecheckInterval recovery in internal/llm/circuit.go + service_funding.go). Two gaps: (1) it only learns a provider is broke AFTER spending a wasted call, and (2) external tooling (the soak harness, an operator pre-flight) cannot determine funding at all because the API key lives in the encrypted keystore. Integrate, WHERE A PROVIDER OFFERS ONE, the provider's balance/credit/usage endpoint to determine funding PROACTIVELY and populate runtime_settings.provider_funding_state without a failed call — e.g. the cloud provider user/balance, OpenRouter /api/v1/auth/key + credits/rate-limit, Provider A usage/billing, Moonshot balance (verify each at design time). Then: pre-skip a known-empty provider in routing before spending a call; surface balance/headroom in `flowforge circuit status` and provider health; and expose a check so an operator (or the soak harness) can confirm a provider is topped up before a long run. 'Wherever possible' is the operative scope: where no balance API exists (e.g. Provider B has no public balance endpoint), fall back to the existing reactive breaker detection. Operator directive 2026-06-16, prompted by being unable to pre-verify cloud-provider funding before a soak.
evidence: internal/llm/circuit.go + internal/llm/service_funding.go (existing REACTIVE funding-aware routing + recovery this complements, not replaces)
runtime_settings.provider_funding_state (durable funding state to populate proactively instead of only on a tripped call)

candidate provider APIs to verify at design time: the cloud provider user/balance, OpenRouter /api/v1/auth/key + credits, Provider A usage/billing, Moonshot balance; Provider B has none -> reactive fallback

items.title: Dedicated Models page: local execution framework + its missing UI + ModelHub model access (with model config decomposed out of monolithic Settings)
items.summary: PRODUCT-VISION ITEM (operator framing 2026-06-17). This is one cohesive effort, not a bolt-on dialog: stand up a DEDICATED MODELS PAGE focused strictly on model management, and in doing so deliver the user base THREE capabilities they do not have by default — (1) access to the highest-performing local model execution framework (the bundled llama.cpp host, embedded-llamacpp-local-llm-host); (2) the management UI that framework is otherwise missing (model list, active/default selection, per-model config, load/unload, health/perf, install/remove — llama.cpp ships no such GUI). POSITIONING: because llama.cpp's native management surface is CLI flags, manual GGUF fetching, and hand-managed server processes, a guided page (compat-gated discovery, one-click install, supervised lifecycle, health/perf surfaced) plausibly makes managing llama.cpp easier and more intuitive through FlowForge than running it standalone — FlowForge as the best way to RUN llama.cpp, not merely a consumer of it; (3) access to the largest collection of local models available, via ModelHub. STRUCTURAL CHANGE this forces: model-related configuration must move OUT of the monolithic Settings page into this focused Models page — Settings stops being a grab-bag for model config, and model management gets a first-class home. Because we are introducing a whole new surface, a deliberate UI/UX REDESIGN is in scope (information architecture for Settings-vs-Models split, the model-management page layout, and the discover→compat-check→download→install→activate flow), not just wiring. The ModelHub integration within this page: (a) search the ModelHub Hub API filtered to GGUF artifacts (library/quant filter), (b) SYSTEM-COMPATIBILITY detection — gate/sort candidates by detected RAM/VRAM, CPU arch, and quantization so a user only installs models their hardware can actually run rather than downloading one that OOMs at load, (c) download with progress surfaced over the existing dashboard WS topics (download-progress events), (d) SHA-pin each installed model the way BundledEmbedModel is pinned so an install is verifiable/reproducible and tamper-evident, (e) hand the installed GGUF to the host via the existing proc.SupervisedProcess serving path, (f) a parallel BYO-GGUF-URL install path for models not on the Hub (consistent with the BYO/self-custody stance elsewhere). HARD CONSTRAINT (operator 2026-06-17): the embedded llama.cpp host is ADDITIVE — support for EXTERNAL / non-embedded local frameworks (a user's own llama.cpp server, Embedded Runtime, any externally-managed local endpoint) must NOT be removed. The Models page manages BOTH the embedded host AND externally-configured local backends as first-class peers; the Settings→Models config decomposition must carry the existing external-endpoint configuration over intact, not drop it in favor of the bundled host. Build order inside the effort: Settings→Models config decomposition + page scaffold and IA redesign first, then the local-host management UI, then the ModelHub discover/download/install flow on top. Depends on embedded-llamacpp-local-llm-host landing first (the host is what serves a downloaded generation model); the embedder sidecar plumbing (supervisor, SHA-pin, WS progress) is the reusable substrate. Settings UI is struct-driven (derives from the Config struct, not TOML) — the model-config move must preserve that invariant, relocating the surface without forking it. STALE-CARD FIX folded in (operator-reported 2026-06-17): the live Settings 'Behavioral Correction - Embedding' card still shows Embedded Runtime framing ('embedded-runtime / embed-model', 'Pulls the model via Embedded Runtime') and NO sidecar running/health state, even though EffectiveEmbeddingEngine resolves to the bundled sidecar (the live engine) and the backend already exposes embedding_engine + Daemon.EmbedderSidecarState(). Rework the card as part of this overhaul: show the effective engine + live sidecar supervisor state, drop the Embedded Runtime copy.

items.title: Per-turn retry-cost ceiling: bound token spend of guard/verifier/react retry cycles and fail-closed on breach (Rule 7)
items.summary: Operator cost directive 2026-06-17 (the cloud provider usage: ~$9 single-day spike on 6/16, June $25.90, 48.1M tokens / 5,289 cloud-provider-v4-pro requests): fruitless retry cycles burn tokens at an alarming rate. A single turn can COMPOUND retry layers — model-attempt fallbacks (routing), react tool loops, guard retries (liveRetryPolicy.MaxRetries=2), and verifier retries — each a fresh provider call. When a turn loops without making progress (same violation repeating, no new evidence), it can multiply paid model calls before reaching natural exhaustion. Today the caps are per-LAYER counts (MaxRetries, react-turn cap), not a per-TURN COST budget across all layers, so the worst-case spend is unbounded in tokens even when bounded in attempts. Add a per-turn token/cost CEILING that, once the turn's accumulated provider spend crosses a threshold, STOPS retrying and fail-closes to an honest blocker (preserving the best content gathered so far), rather than continuing to spend. This is Rule 7 made enforceable: 'token budgets are not advisory — surface the breach, do not silently overrun.' Substrate already exists: inference_costs records per-attempt token/cost, turn_diagnostics aggregates per-turn (RCA Rule 14), and the guard-exhaustion machinery (guardExhaustionMustFailClosed, blockedOutcomeForGuardExhaustion, decideGuardRetryAfterProgress) already has a fail-closed honest-blocker path to reuse — the ceiling becomes one more exhaustion trigger. Report BOTH quantitative (per-turn token/cost spent, retries triggered, ceiling-breach count) and qualitative (which retry layer dominates spend) per the standing measurement preference. Correctness link: a behavioral defect that yields a bad answer triggers verifier retries downstream, so fixing correctness defects (e.g. the durable-history-drop confabulation) directly cuts fruitless-retry burn — cost and correctness are the same lever. Pairs with preemptive-shaping (a guard firing/retrying means shaping already failed) and the provider-funding-state work (pre-skip empty providers before spending a call). Scope to settle at planning: the ceiling value (fixed vs config vs per-model), whether it is a hard stop or a soft warn-then-stop, how it composes with the existing per-layer caps, and how the operator surfaces/overrides it.
evidence: src/engine guard/verifier retry machinery (liveRetryPolicy.MaxRetries, decideGuardRetryAfterProgress, runVerifierPass) — per-layer attempt caps, no per-turn cost budget across layers
inference_costs table (per-attempt token/cost) + turn_diagnostics aggregation (Rule 14 substrate the ceiling reads to know spend mid-turn)
guardExhaustionMustFailClosed / blockedOutcomeForGuardExhaustion (existing fail-closed honest-blocker path the ceiling reuses as a new exhaustion trigger)
AGENT_GUIDELINES.md Rule 7: token budgets are not advisory — surface the breach, do not silently overrun

items.title: v1.2.1 comprehensive answer-fidelity audit: the framework must never discard, corrupt, or degrade a correct model answer
items.summary: STATUS 2026-06-17: the static-sweep audit (6 dimensions) and its ~20 fixes across 6 systemic roots (persistence seam, recovery substance-gate, raw-tool-output filter, compression fact-preservation, swap/finalize instrumentation, #35) were EXECUTED IN v1.2.0 — pulled in BEFORE the tag because the operator reclassified this from a future-audit concern to an IDENTIFIED BEHAVIORAL DEFECT (behavioral-correctness-paramount: a known defect class is a now-item that outranks release ceremony; you do not ship with it live even when the surface soak is green). The v1.2.1 REMAINDER scoped here is the permanent regression guard the static fixes still lack: the ADVERSARIAL 'correct-answer-survival' soak harness (the inverse of normal soaks — feed conditions where the model produces a CORRECT answer that a guard/recovery/finalize might wrongly degrade, assert it survives) plus any low-priority leftovers (e.g. uniform direct-stage content_head instrumentation). Original framing follows. Operator directive 2026-06-17 ('plan a comprehensive audit for this sort of crap'). The v1.2.0 promote loop surfaced not three one-off bugs but a CLASS: the framework discarding, corrupting, or degrading a CORRECT model answer somewhere between generation and delivery. Instances proven this cycle, each with recorded-state RCA: (1) long_mixed durable-history drop — a correct direct-stage reply was returned to the client but NEVER persisted to session_messages (the direct-stage finalize bypassed the persist seam), so a later turn confabulated off the hole; (2) geopolitical mode B — the model returned empty at reflect and the empty-response fallback (synthesizeFromToolResults) DUMPED raw Playwright/browser tool noise (`### Ran Playwright code … [ref=e2]`) verbatim as the answer; (3) geopolitical mode A — the model produced a correct 3285-char sitrep, the execution_truth web-read grounding guard FALSE-POSITIVED (its markers were raw-XML/URL tokens a synthesis paraphrases away), and the recovery OVERWROTE the good answer with a degenerate `From <url>: <url>` projection. Two roots unify them: (a) MANY independent 'what ships to the user' selectors with inconsistent rules (loop finalizeContent + max-turns/idle/deadline terminal returns + reflect commit + pipeline projections/recoveries/finalize + direct-stage outcomes + guard-exhaustion fallbacks + stream finalize), and (b) RAW TOOL OUTPUT corrupting downstream logic (browser snapshots, RSS XML, transport status, accessibility [ref=eN] refs, bare URLs) — the compression-corruption family. This is the operator's preemptive-shaping thesis in the flesh: a guard/recovery firing WRONGLY is worse than not firing — it destroys correct output AND (per the cost lens) bills paid retries to do it. AUDIT DIMENSIONS: (1) TERMINAL-CONTENT SELECTORS — statically enumerate EVERY path that decides what ships, and assert each: persists to durable history, rejects degenerate/raw-tool-noise content (via the shared agenttools.ToolOutputLacksReadableContent detector), never silently discards a correct model answer, and is Rule-14 instrumented (the reflection_finalized / loop_terminal_selection pattern). (2) OVERRIDE/RECOVERY PATHS — every guard, projection, and recovery that can REPLACE the model's answer; audit each for false-positive risk (brittle marker/pattern presence checks firing on correct syntheses) and for 'recovery worse than original' (a fail-closed/projection answer that degrades a good one). (3) RAW-TOOL-OUTPUT LEAKAGE — every synthesis/projection/recovery seam centralizes noise-filtering; no raw browser/XML/transport/accessibility/URL content reaches the user as an answer (folds in the pending [ref=eN] leak item). (4) PERSISTENCE SEAMS — every reply path provably writes to durable history (long_mixed class), with no silent-swallow on contention. (5) COMPRESSION/CONTEXT-REDUCTION SEAMS — every lossy step (TOTOF collapse, prompt-compression, context checkpoints, CompactBeforeArchive, topic summaries, memory consolidation) ships WITH a fact-preservation proof. (6) RULE-14 COMPLETENESS — every content-decision point records what it selected/transformed/discarded so the next occurrence is auto-legible. METHODOLOGY: static enumeration (grep/AST sweep of selectors/recoveries/projections/synthesis/persistence sites) + an ADVERSARIAL 'correct-answer-survival' soak that probes the INVERSE of normal soaks — feed the agent conditions where it produces a CORRECT answer that a guard/recovery/finalize might wrongly degrade (raw-XML sources, browser-snapshot-only evidence, paraphrasing syntheses, back-to-back same-session turns) and assert the good answer SURVIVES — plus a fact-preservation test per compression seam. DELIVERABLE: a severity-ranked findings report feeding v1.2.1 scope, in the spirit of the cycle-opening triple-lens audit but targeted at answer fidelity. The Rule-14 reflect/terminal instrumentation already deployed in v1.2.0 is the substrate this audit reads.
evidence: v1.2.0 RCAs (recorded-state): long_mixed durable-history drop (direct-stage persist bypass), geopolitical mode B (empty-reflect raw-tool-noise dump), geopolitical mode A (execution_truth grounding false-positive + degenerate recovery overwrite)

items.title: Investigate GraphRAG (hippocampal-indexing knowledge-graph + PageRank associative retrieval) for the memory system
items.summary: INVESTIGATION (operator-provided reference 2026-06-17: https://reference.example.com). GraphRAG / GraphRAG-2 is a neurobiologically-inspired long-term-memory framework for LLMs, modeled on the hippocampal-indexing theory of human memory: an LLM extracts a lightweight KNOWLEDGE GRAPH from passages, and at retrieval time the system runs GRAPH TRAVERSAL (Personalized PageRank in the original GraphRAG, seeded by the query's entities) to perform ASSOCIATIVE, MULTI-HOP, cross-document retrieval in a SINGLE step — surfacing related information even when terminology does not match, linking CONCEPTS rather than keywords. How it differs: vs standard vector RAG (embedding/keyword match with heavy upfront index + storage overhead) it trades the vector index for a graph and claims lower indexing overhead + the ability to ingest streaming/live data without index rebuilds; vs iterative multi-hop RAG (several LLM retrieve-read round-trips) it resolves the multi-hop association in one graph step. Reported benefits: associative concept-linking, multi-hop QA, model-agnostic (any LLM). WHY THIS IS WORTH OUR TIME: flowforge ALREADY has the substrate this technique needs — a hippocampus surface (internal/db hippocampus), knowledge_facts + internal/agent/memory/retrieval_tiers_graph.go, semantic_memory, memory consolidation (consolidation_phases.go), and a pinned/measured embedder foundation — so the question is concrete: does a GraphRAG-style KG+PPR associative retrieval beat our current tiered retrieval on MULTI-HOP recall, and at what cost (LLM triple-extraction per ingested passage, a graph store, PPR compute, and the contradiction/consolidation interactions)? Investigation scope: (1) read the method (GraphRAG + GraphRAG-2 papers/repo, not just the blog) and map it onto our existing knowledge_facts graph + retrieval tiers; (2) decide BUILD (adopt the framework) vs BORROW (run Personalized PageRank over the knowledge_facts graph we already build, seeded by query entities, as an additional retrieval tier) vs PASS; (3) a small multi-hop-recall benchmark comparing current retrieval vs a PPR-over-knowledge_facts prototype on representative associative queries. DELIVERABLE: a reviewed comparison + the benchmark with BOTH a quantitative score (multi-hop recall/precision, ingest + retrieval cost) AND a qualitative read (retrieval quality, failure modes, fit with consolidation/contradiction-detection), and a build/borrow/pass recommendation — per the standing quantitative-AND-qualitative preference. Pairs with the embedder-foundation work (retrieval quality depends on the embedder) and the agent-owned knowledge-graph/Obsidian vault item (shared graph substrate).
evidence: operator-provided reference 2026-06-17: https://reference.example.com (GraphRAG/GraphRAG-2: hippocampal-indexing KG + Personalized PageRank associative multi-hop retrieval; model-agnostic; trades vector index for a graph)

existing memory substrate to map onto / borrow into: internal/db hippocampus, knowledge_facts, internal/agent/memory/retrieval_tiers_graph.go, semantic_memory, consolidation_phases.go (contradiction detection + consolidation)
embedder-foundation dependency: retrieval quality is embedder-bound (see project_embedder_foundation) — benchmark on the pinned embedder

BORROW candidate: run Personalized PageRank over the knowledge_facts graph we ALREADY build, seeded by query entities, as an additional associative retrieval tier — before committing to adopting the whole framework
deliverable per standing preference: multi-hop-recall benchmark with quantitative score AND qualitative read + a build/borrow/pass recommendation

items.title: Release gates on RESOLUTION of every behavioral soak failure, not on the severity tier — attribution_only is not a license to ship
items.summary: Operator directive 2026-06-17. The behavior-soak severity tiers (release_gate / attribution_only / stress_only) currently double as a GATING policy: only release_gate F's block the tag; attribution_only/stress_only F's are recorded but waved through. That is a defect-hiding anti-pattern, proven this cycle: geopolitical_sitrep was attribution_only and was repeatedly called 'doesn't gate', then turned out to be a DETERMINISTIC framework defect (the loop discarding a correct answer + the grounding guard false-positiving). The tier PRE-ATTRIBUTES a failure to 'not cleanly attributable' BEFORE any RCA — the exact Rule 17 anti-pattern (an unfalsifiable variance story) baked into the taxonomy. CHANGE THE POLICY: the release gates on RESOLUTION of EVERY behavioral failure regardless of tier — (a) every F, any tier, must be diagnosed to a recorded-state conclusion before release; (b) RCA proves a DEFECT -> fix it (gates); (c) RCA proves a GENUINE EXTERNALITY -> it must be EVIDENCED (captured bytes show e.g. the live source was empty, not 'probably flaky') and cleared by a STABILITY CRITERION (green on re-run / N-of-M), documented, never a standing exemption. The tier KEEPS its real job — PRIORITY and diagnosis routing, and choosing the stability bar for genuinely stochastic (live-web / high model_sensitivity) scenarios — but it no longer grants permission to ship a failure. Implementation in scripts/run-agent-behavior-soak.py + the release gate: replace 'only release_gate F gates' with 'any F gates until resolved'; add a per-scenario stability/re-run policy for scenarios flagged stochastic, with the externality evidence required to clear them; surface unresolved F's (any tier) as a hard release blocker. This is the methodology pair to behavioral-correctness-paramount and Rule 17 — see feedback_no_severity_tier_excuses.
evidence: scripts/run-agent-behavior-soak.py severity tiers (release_gate / attribution_only / stress_only) + the release gate that only blocks on release_gate F's
proof the tier hid a real defect: geopolitical_sitrep (attribution_only) was a deterministic framework defect (answer-fidelity class), not variance
Rule 17 / feedback_diagnose_from_recorded_state: never attribute a failure to variance/external without recorded-state evidence — the tier does this by default
stability criterion (green on re-run / N-of-M) for genuinely stochastic scenarios replaces label-based exemption; externality must be evidenced (captured bytes), not assumed
operator directive 2026-06-17: 'why should I undervalue attribution_only … perhaps we should be gating on attribution' — see feedback_no_severity_tier_excuses

items.title: Intelligent parallel compaction engine: sift chaff from wheat OFF the critical path, routing the wheat to long-term persistence AND runtime context reduction
items.summary: ARCHITECTURE (operator vision 2026-06-17). Today's compaction is the wrong mechanism: the compact() ladder (internal/agent/context_extract.go) runs SYNCHRONOUSLY on the turn's critical path and decides signal-vs-noise with crude heuristic scoring (salient-sentence / topic-extract / skeleton, scoring digits as a bonus). It is structurally incapable of doing this well and proved it twice in the v1.2.0 audit cycle: it DROPPED a load-bearing count under budget pressure (the long_mixed / continuity confabulation class), and the audit's hard-keep fix then OVER-corrected — preserving whole tool messages verbatim at the skeleton stage bloated the context and evicted OTHER recall content (the continuity_benchmark_threshold_recall regression). Replace it with an INTELLIGENT, METHODICAL compaction engine that runs IN PARALLEL to the user/agent/model pipeline (async, off the critical path — like the existing post-turn ingest and memory consolidation), sifts the textual CHAFF (filler, redundant prose, social niceties, transport/tool noise) from the WHEAT (salient facts, decisions, entities, quantitative values, IDs, open questions, the user's actual obligations), and routes the wheat to TWO destinations from one extraction: (1) LONG-TERM PERSISTENCE — durable memory (knowledge_facts / semantic_memory / the agent knowledge graph; pairs with investigate-graph-rag-associative-memory-retrieval and consolidation_phases.go), and (2) RUNTIME OVERHEAD REDUCTION — a compact, fact-preserving context representation the live turn consumes, so the model sees wheat, not chaff. KEY PROPERTIES: 'intelligent' = a CHEAP model pass and/or structured extraction (not the scoring ladder) that GUARANTEES the load-bearing content survives — the IsLoadBearingToken / fact-preservation floor from the answer-fidelity audit is a HARD invariant the intelligent layer may never violate (it can drop chaff freely, never a fact). 'Parallel' = a background worker that produces a compacted ARTIFACT future turns reuse (amortized: compact once, reuse across turns) so compaction adds ZERO turn latency, its model-cost is amortized, and because it SHRINKS the live context it REDUCES per-turn token cost — it pays for itself (the cost lens, per-turn-retry-cost-ceiling). THE UNIFICATION: runtime context compaction and long-term memory consolidation are the SAME 'extract wheat from chaff' operation with different destinations — this engine is the single seam; it extends/absorbs consolidation_phases.go rather than duplicating it, and composes with (does not re-implement) the TOTOF reflection collapse. Motivated by the v1.2.0 compaction regression; the immediate v1.2.0 fix (hard-keep facts in the existing ladder without the bloat) is a STOPGAP — this is the real mechanism. MINIMUM-VIABLE FIRST INCREMENT (operator 2026-06-17): on EVERY turn, reduce the USER REQUEST and the MODEL RESPONSE to their most pertinent elements — proactive per-turn distillation, applied uniformly rather than only reactively under budget pressure, so the working context stays lean from the start and the crude under-pressure ladder rarely fires. Critical constraints on the minimum increment: the DURABLE record (session_messages, what the user actually saw) stays FULL — only the context representation carried into FUTURE turns is distilled; the user-request reduction must preserve the actual obligation AND any exact-literal requirement (reply-only-literal contracts, injection-resistance where the exact bytes matter) — never paraphrase away a literal the turn depends on; and the same fact-preservation floor applies to both (the model response's distilled form must keep the facts/decisions a later turn will recall — the continuity class). This increment alone delivers most of the runtime-overhead-reduction benefit and is the natural place to start. THIRD LAYER — the DREAM MECHANISM (operator 2026-06-17). 'Dream mechanism' is the project's name for the PERIODIC IDLE-TIME memory-hygiene job (the off-path ConsolidationPipeline, internal/agent/memory/consolidation_phases.go, which already runs hygiene phases like phaseWithinTierDedup). Its importance is chiefly for the PERSISTENCE layer: when the compacted content tokenizes into 'facts', we do NOT want to redundantly STORE a fact that is really a restatement/instance of an EXISTING LEARNING — that recurrence should REINFORCE the existing learning (strengthen its confidence/recency/weight, or merge), not pile up as a duplicate memory. This is associative REINFORCEMENT, richer than exact dedup: repeated exposure strengthens the memory (and supersession applies — a later fact obsoletes an earlier one, per the prefer-latest-value rule). The reduction is LOSSLESS IN RELEVANT CONTENT — it preserves every relevant fact/learning (the wheat) and may drop only redundant/irrelevant STORAGE; 'lossless' is scoped to relevance, not bytes. The two passes are a two-speed, biologically-faithful consolidation: a FAST per-turn distillation (lossy-but-fact-preserving — drops chaff) feeding the SLOW dream pass (lossless-in-relevant-content — reinforces existing learnings instead of re-storing, dedups, supersedes, never drops a relevant fact). This cleanly separates the two reduction kinds so the aggressive (lossy) one is fact-floored and the hygiene (relevance-lossless) one can be trusted to never lose a learning. It extends the dream/consolidation pipeline with a 'reinforce-don't-redundantly-store' phase over the compacted content rather than a new subsystem. Scope to settle at planning: the extraction model (cheap local vs the turn model), the artifact schema (facts/decisions/entities/open-issues), staleness handling (a turn arriving before compaction completes uses the last artifact, verbatim history otherwise — never a half-built compaction), how it composes with TOTOF + consolidation without duplicating, and the fact-preservation floor as a non-negotiable invariant validated by the compression fact-preservation tests.
evidence: internal/agent/context_extract.go (the synchronous heuristic compact() ladder being replaced) + context_request.go selectHistory (budget-driven eviction)

fact-preservation floor (IsLoadBearingToken + the compression fact-preservation test tables) is the HARD invariant the intelligent layer may not violate
pairs with investigate-graph-rag-associative-memory-retrieval (KG extraction from passages is the persistence side of the same operation) and TOTOF reflection (compose, do not duplicate)
